elements.sent-2411-001: This article introduces a novel family of optimization algorithms—Anisotropic Gaussian Smoothing Gradient Descent (AGS-GD), AGS-Stochastic Gradient Descent (AGS-SGD), and AGS-Adam—that employ anisotropic Gaussian smoothing to enhance traditional gradient-based methods, including GD, SGD, and Adam.
elements.sent-2411-002: The primary goal of these approaches is to address the challenge of optimization methods becoming trapped in suboptimal local minima by replacing the standard gradient with a non-local gradient derived from averaging function values using anisotropic Gaussian smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-003: Unlike isotropic Gaussian smoothing (IGS), AGS adapts the smoothing directionality based on the properties of the underlying function, aligning better with complex loss landscapes and improving convergence.
elements.sent-2411-004: The anisotropy is computed by adjusting the covariance matrix of the Gaussian distribution, allowing for directional smoothing tailored to the gradient's behavior.
elements.sent-2411-005: This technique mitigates the impact of minor fluctuations, enabling the algorithms to approach global minima more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-006: We provide detailed convergence analyses that extend the results from both the original (unsmoothed) methods and the IGS case to the more general anisotropic smoothing, applicable to both convex and non-convex, L-smooth functions.
elements.sent-2411-007: In the stochastic setting, these algorithms converge to a noisy ball, with its size determined by the smoothing parameters.
elements.sent-2411-008: The article also outlines the theoretical benefits of anisotropic smoothing and details its practical implementation using Monte Carlo estimation, aligning with established zero-order optimization techniques.
elements.sent-2411-009: A standard approach to solving optimization problems is to use gradient descent (GD) when the function is deterministic and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) or adaptive moment estimation (Adam) in the stochastic setting.
elements.sent-2411-010: A common problem with these methods is that the path that they take can lead them into suboptimal basins that they cannot escape from.
elements.sent-2411-011: The focus of this paper is to provide alternative optimization techniques that avoid this behavior.
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, we exchange the gradient of the function in GD, SGD, and Adam with a non-local gradient that comes from averaging out the values of the original function.
elements.sent-2411-013: The resulting averaging function has the property that small fluctuations in the original function are smoothed out, which make gradient descent algorithms less likely to find non-global minima.
elements.sent-2411-014: We refer to the anisotropic Gaussian smoothing algorithms modifying GD, SGD, and Adam as AGS-GD, AGS-SGD, and AGS-Adam, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-015: Our goal is to minimize real-valued functions on $\mathbb{R ^d$, that is, we aim to solve the optimization problem \begin{equation}\label{eqn:min_of_f} \min_{\bmx\in\mathbb{R}^d}f(\bmx).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-016: We consider both the deterministic and stochastic settings.
elements.sent-2411-017: In the stochastic setting, we are given $F:\mathbb{R ^d\times\Omega\to\mathbb{R}$ for some probability space $\Omega$ and a sample $\{\omega_1,...,\omega_K\}\subseteq\Omega$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: We denote \begin{equation}f_{k}(\bmx)=F(\bmx,\omega_k):\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}\end{equation} and assume $f:\mathbb{R ^d\to\mathbb{R}$ satisfies \begin{equation}\mathbb{E}\big[f_k(\bmx)\big]=f(\bmx) \text{ and } \mathbb{E}\big[\nabla f_k(\bmx)\big]=\nabla f(\bmx).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The stochastic variant of this problem is the focus of machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-020: For any function $g:\mathbb{R ^d\to\mathbb{R}$ and symmetric, invertible matrix $\Sigma\in\mathbb{R ^{d\times d}$, we define the $\Sigma$-Gaussian smoothed version of $g$ as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:definition_of_fsigma} g_{\Sigma}(\bmx) =\frac{1}{\pi^{\frac{d}{2}}}\int_{R^d}g\left(\bmx+\Sigma\bmu\right)e^{-\|\bmu\|^2}\;d\bmu.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: There are two other convenient and equivalent formulations of $g_{\Sigma}$, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:change_of_variables} g_{\Sigma}(\bmx) =\frac{1}{\pi^{\frac{d}{2}}|\Sigma|}\int_{R^d}g(\bmv)e^{-(\bmv-\bmx)^T\Sigma^{-2}(\bmv-\bmx)}\;d\bmv =(g\star k_{\Sigma})(\bmx)\end{equation} where $|\Sigma|$ denotes the determinant of $\Sigma$, $\star$ denotes convolution, and \begin{equation}k_{\Sigma}(\bmx)=\frac{1}{\pi^{\frac{d}{2}}|\Sigma|} e^{-\bmx^T\Sigma^{-2}\bmx}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-022: From these formulations, as long as $f$ is nice enough, the gradient can be computed~as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:gradient_equation} \nabla g_{\Sigma}(\bmx) =\frac{2}{\pi^{\frac{d}{2}}}\Sigma^{-1}\int_{R^d}\bmu g\left(\bmx+\Sigma\bmu\right)e^{-\|\bmu\|^2}\;d\bmu,\end{equation} where we first pass the gradient into the integral~\eqref{eqn:change_of_variables and the take the gradient of $k_{\Sigma}$.}

elements.sent-2411-188: In this effort we introduced a family of anisotropic Gaussian smoothing algorithms, AGS-GD, AGS-SGD, and AGS-Adam, as alternatives to traditional gradient descent optimization methods.
elements.sent-2411-189: These algorithms address a major challenge in optimization: the tendency of methods to become trapped in suboptimal local minima.
elements.sent-2411-190: By replacing the standard gradient with a non-local gradient derived through anisotropic Gaussian smoothing, we provide a more effective path toward convergence.
elements.sent-2411-191: Our convergence analyses build on and extend existing results for standard gradient descent and isotropic Gaussian smoothing, demonstrating their applicability to both convex and non-convex, L-smooth functions.
elements.sent-2411-192: In the stochastic setting, we showed that these algorithms converge to a noisy ball around a minimum, with the size of this ball determined by the smoothing parameters—emphasizing the importance of parameter selection in practice.
elements.sent-2411-193: While the theoretical benefits of anisotropic Gaussian smoothing are clear, practical implementation remains a challenge due to the computational complexity of calculating smoothed functions or their gradients.
elements.sent-2411-194: To mitigate this, we utilize Monte Carlo estimation to approximate the smoothed gradient, aligning with established zero-order optimization methods and making the AGS algorithms more feasible for real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-195: Our work primarily establishes the theoretical foundation and convergence properties of these anisotropic Gaussian smoothing algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-196: One particularly useful research direction would be investigating the relationship between smoothing parameter selection and algorithm performance across different problem domains.
elements.sent-2411-197: We believe that AGS algorithms have significant potential to advance optimization by offering robust and efficient solutions for complex optimization tasks.. \bibliographystyle{plain .}
